title: Pricing Practices & Price Discrimination
simple_html:
The simple textbook rule is "set one price where MR = MC." Real firms rarely do that. Instead they use pricing practices — clever ways to charge different customers different amounts, or to bundle and structure prices — to capture more of the value buyers place on a product.

The big idea is price discrimination: selling the same (or nearly the same) good at different prices that are not justified by cost differences. A cinema charging students less, an airline charging more for last-minute seats, a software firm selling a "pro" tier — all are extracting extra revenue from customers willing and able to pay more.

The three classic degrees

  • First-degree (perfect): charge each buyer the maximum they'd pay. Rare, but auctions and individual haggling come close.
  • Second-degree: price varies by quantity or version chosen — bulk discounts, tiered plans, "good/better/best." Customers sort themselves.
  • Third-degree: charge different identifiable groups different prices — student, senior, geographic, or peak vs. off-peak.

For discrimination to work, three conditions must hold: the firm needs some market power, it must be able to separate customers by willingness to pay, and it must prevent resale (arbitrage) between them.

Key takeaways

  • Price discrimination turns "consumer surplus" (value buyers keep) into firm revenue.
  • It needs market power, segmentation, and no resale.
  • Charge lower prices to more price-sensitive (elastic) groups.
  • Other tools — two-part tariffs, bundling, and markup pricing — achieve similar goals.
intermediate_html:
Beyond the single-price MR = MC rule lies a toolkit of pricing practices firms use to capture more surplus and manage demand. The centerpiece is price discrimination: charging different prices for the same good where the price gap does not reflect a cost difference. Its purpose is to convert consumer surplus (the gap between what buyers would pay and what they do pay) into producer revenue.

Preconditions for price discrimination

  • Market power — a downward-sloping demand curve; a price taker cannot discriminate.
  • Segmentation — the firm can identify or induce groups with different willingness to pay.
  • No arbitrage — low-price buyers cannot resell to high-price buyers (easier for services than for goods).

The three degrees

First-degree charges each unit at the buyer's maximum reservation price, capturing all surplus; output reaches the efficient level but the firm takes everything. Second-degree prices by quantity or version, letting customers self-select (block tariffs, tiered subscriptions, versioning). Third-degree charges distinct prices to separable groups (age, location, time).

The third-degree rule and a worked example

To maximize profit across segments, equate marginal revenue across markets to common marginal cost: MR₁ = MR₂ = MC. Using the relation MR = P(1 + 1/ε), this implies the inverse-elasticity rule: the segment with more elastic (price-sensitive) demand gets the lower price. Formally, P₁ / P₂ = (1 + 1/ε₂) / (1 + 1/ε₁).

Example. A firm has MC = 10. Adults have demand P = 50 − Q_a; students have P = 30 − Q_s. For adults: MR = 50 − 2Q_a = 10 → Q_a = 20, P_a = 30. For students: MR = 30 − 2Q_s = 10 → Q_s = 10, P_s = 20. The more price-sensitive students pay 20, adults pay 30 — same product, no cost difference. Total profit beats any single price.

Other pricing practices

PracticeMechanismExample
Two-part tariffFixed access fee + per-unit price (set per-unit near MC, capture surplus via the fee)Club membership, theme parks, Costco
BundlingSell goods together to reduce dispersion in willingness to payOffice software suite, cable packages
Peak-load pricingHigher price when capacity is strainedElectricity, surge ride-hailing
Markup (cost-plus) pricingPrice = AVC × (1 + markup); optimal markup = −1/(ε+1)Retail, restaurants

How to apply it

  • Estimate elasticities by segment — then price the elastic segment lower and the inelastic segment higher.
  • Build a fence — use student IDs, geography, timing, or versioning to keep segments apart and block resale.
  • Use a two-part tariff when usage is repeated — price the marginal unit at MC for efficient volume, recover profit through the access fee.
  • Bundle when valuations are negatively correlated across products — it smooths willingness to pay and lifts total revenue.

The unifying logic: every practice is an attempt to move the firm's outcome closer to first-degree discrimination — extracting the value each buyer is genuinely willing to part with.
full_html:
Single-price profit maximization (MR = MC) is a special case. In practice, firms with any market power deploy a rich set of pricing practices to capture a larger share of the surplus that a single price leaves on the table. The intellectual core is price discrimination, formalized by Arthur Pigou (Pigou, 1920), who introduced the still-standard taxonomy of first-, second-, and third-degree discrimination.

Definition and preconditions

Price discrimination is charging different prices for the same good where the differential is not attributable to cost. (Charging more to ship farther is not discrimination; charging seniors less for an identical seat is.) Three conditions are necessary: (1) market power — a downward-sloping firm demand curve; (2) segmentation — the ability to identify or induce buyers with differing reservation prices; and (3) no arbitrage — prevention of resale from low- to high-price buyers. Services and non-transferable goods (haircuts, electricity, electronically licensed software) are easier to discriminate on than storable, tradeable commodities.

The three degrees

First-degree (perfect) discrimination. The firm charges each unit at the buyer's exact reservation price, appropriating the entire consumer surplus. Output expands to the allocatively efficient point where P = MC on the last unit — paradoxically, perfect discrimination produces the efficient quantity, but distributes all gains to the seller. Approximations: individualized negotiation, first-price auctions, and increasingly, algorithmic personalized pricing using rich customer data.

Second-degree discrimination (nonlinear pricing). The firm cannot observe a buyer's type but designs a menu that induces self-selection: block/quantity discounts, tiered subscriptions, and versioning (good/better/best). The design problem is one of mechanism design under hidden information — the menu must satisfy incentive-compatibility so high-value buyers do not choose the low-end option (Maskin & Riley, 1984). Damaged-goods and fences (e.g., Saturday-night-stay airfares) are deliberate quality degradation to separate segments.

Third-degree discrimination (group pricing). The firm separates buyers into observable groups (age, location, time, channel) and sets a different price in each. This is the most common form and has a clean optimality condition.

The third-degree optimum and the inverse-elasticity rule

With common marginal cost MC across segments, profit is maximized where marginal revenue is equalized across markets and set to marginal cost: MR₁ = MR₂ = ... = MC. Since MR = P(1 + 1/ε) (with ε the own-price elasticity, a negative number), it follows that P_i (1 + 1/ε_i) = MC for each segment, giving the inverse-elasticity rule: P_i = MC / (1 + 1/ε_i). The more elastic the segment (|ε| large), the lower the price; the more inelastic, the higher the markup. This is the same Lerner logic from monopoly (Lesson 6) applied segment by segment.

Worked calculation

A monopolist with constant MC = 20 serves two separable markets: business demand P_b = 100 − Q_b (inelastic-leaning) and leisure demand P_l = 60 − Q_l. Business: MR_b = 100 − 2Q_b = 20 → Q_b = 40, P_b = 60. Leisure: MR_l = 60 − 2Q_l = 20 → Q_l = 20, P_l = 40. Discriminating profit (ignoring fixed cost) is (60 − 20)·40 + (40 − 20)·20 = 1,600 + 400 = 2,000. A single uniform price would yield less, illustrating why segmentation pays. Note the higher price falls on the less elastic (business) segment — exactly the inverse-elasticity prediction.

Beyond classic discrimination: the broader toolkit

  • Two-part tariff. An access fee A plus a per-unit price p (Oi, 1971, "A Disneyland Dilemma"). With identical consumers the firm sets p = MC (efficient consumption) and extracts all surplus through A — replicating first-degree discrimination. With heterogeneous consumers the firm trades off a higher fee (which loses small buyers) against a higher per-unit price; the optimum sets p > MC.
  • Bundling. Stigler (1963) showed that selling goods as a bundle raises profit when buyers' valuations are negatively correlated, because bundling reduces the dispersion of total willingness to pay. Mixed bundling (offering both the bundle and components separately) typically dominates pure bundling (Adams & Yellen, 1976).
  • Peak-load pricing. When capacity is fixed and demand fluctuates, efficient prices charge peak users the full marginal capacity cost and off-peak users only short-run marginal cost (Williamson, 1966). Used in electricity, telecom, and dynamic "surge" ride-hailing.
  • Cost-plus / markup pricing. The common managerial heuristic P = AVC × (1 + m). It is consistent with profit maximization when the markup is set by the elasticity: optimal markup m* = −1/(ε + 1), again the Lerner relation in disguise. The danger is using a fixed markup that ignores elasticity changes.
  • Transfer pricing. Internal pricing between divisions; the profit-maximizing transfer price for an intermediate good with no external market is its marginal cost (links to Cost Theory, Lesson 5).

Welfare and policy debates

Price discrimination is ambiguous for welfare. Third-degree discrimination usually raises the firm's profit but its effect on total output and welfare depends on whether it opens new markets that a uniform price would not serve (Schmalensee, 1981; Varian, 1985). If discrimination expands output to otherwise-unserved customers (e.g., low-income or developing-country pricing of pharmaceuticals), it can be welfare-improving despite the inequity in surplus distribution. First-degree discrimination is allocatively efficient but distributionally extreme. Legally, discrimination is constrained in some jurisdictions (e.g., the U.S. Robinson-Patman Act for goods sold to competing resellers) and increasingly scrutinized in the context of algorithmic personalized pricing, which can approach first-degree discrimination and raises fairness and privacy concerns.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Calling cost-based price differences discrimination. Differential prices that mirror differential costs are not discrimination.
  • Ignoring arbitrage. Discrimination collapses if low-price buyers resell; many real schemes are really about building resale fences.
  • Pricing the inelastic segment too low. The inverse-elasticity rule is counterintuitive to managers who want to "reward loyal" (often inelastic) customers with discounts.
  • Treating markup as fixed. A constant markup is optimal only if elasticity is constant; demand shifts should move the markup.
  • Confusing the two-part tariff's parts. The per-unit price should be near MC, not high; profit comes from the access fee.

In sum, every advanced pricing practice is a device to push the firm from a single uniform price toward perfect price discrimination — capturing more of each buyer's willingness to pay. Mastery requires linking these tools back to elasticity (Lesson 2), market power (Lesson 6: Market Structures), and, where rivals react to your menu, to strategic interaction (Lesson 8: Game Theory).
